articles.title: Validate HSS authentication vectors before changing SIP policy
articles.summary: Many authentication failures come from bad or stale vectors rather than front-end SIP logic.
content: When to use: REGISTER requests are stuck in repeated 401 or 407 exchanges and subscribers never advance to a stable registration.
Action: Confirm the HSS or UDM is returning fresh AKA vectors for the affected users and verify the vector cache is not serving stale material.
Why it works: Without valid vectors, every downstream SIP retry is just replaying a challenge that cannot succeed.
Verify: Successful challenge completion rises and repeated challenge loops per subscriber drop quickly.
Capture: Example public identities, vector age, and the dependency path between the SIP tier and the subscriber store.

articles.title: Correct nonce expiration drift across authentication nodes
articles.summary: Time drift or mismatched nonce TTL values can invalidate otherwise good credentials.
content: When to use: Clients respond to a challenge, but the next hop still rejects the response as stale or invalid.
Action: Compare nonce lifetime settings and time synchronization across the nodes participating in the challenge flow.
Why it works: Authentication depends on strict timing and replay windows, so drift between nodes can break the exchange without any credential error.
Verify: Fresh challenges remain valid across retries and authentication failures stop clustering around stale nonce messages.
Guardrail: Fix the clock or TTL mismatch first instead of extending nonce lifetime beyond acceptable security limits.

articles.title: Restore Diameter connectivity for the authentication backend
articles.summary: Authentication loops often begin when the SIP tier loses a clean path to Diameter or subscriber services.
content: When to use: The SIP edge challenges correctly, but backend lookups time out or fail when fetching subscriber state.
Action: Check connectivity, TLS state, and peer health for the Diameter or backend subscriber path used by the auth service.
Why it works: SIP challenge loops persist when the frontend cannot complete the backend lookup needed to validate the subscriber.
Verify: Backend lookup latency falls and authentication completions recover without forcing a mass restart.
Escalate if: Multiple backend peers are degraded, which suggests a broader platform or network incident.

articles.title: Repair incorrect subscriber credentials or profile state
articles.summary: A bad subscriber profile can look like a system-wide auth outage if it affects a large imported batch.
content: When to use: Authentication failures cluster around a specific subscriber group or after a recent import or sync event.
Action: Compare the failing subscriber records with a known-good profile, then correct keys, realm data, or service entitlements as needed.
Why it works: Fixing the data defect removes the repeated challenge loop without destabilizing the shared auth service.
Verify: The corrected cohort registers normally and failure volume for unaffected users remains low.
Documentation: Record which profile field was wrong so future RCA output can distinguish data issues from platform faults.

articles.title: Rotate stale authentication secrets after a controlled verification
articles.summary: Stale or partially rolled secrets can break challenge validation across replicas.
content: When to use: Authentication failures start immediately after a secret update, config rollout, or certificate replacement.
Action: Verify all replicas are reading the same secret revision, then rotate or roll back the auth secret in a coordinated way.
Why it works: Mixed secret state across nodes produces intermittent failures that are hard to explain from SIP traces alone.
Verify: Errors stop correlating with one pod or one revision and challenge success normalizes across the fleet.
Risk: Secret rotation changes a security boundary, so apply it with a clear rollback and audit trail.

articles.title: Inspect the AKA challenge loop instead of retrying the same REGISTER flow
articles.summary: Repeated AKA failures usually reveal a deterministic mismatch that retries will not fix.
content: When to use: The same subscriber repeatedly cycles through challenge and reject responses with no sign of progress.
Action: Trace one end-to-end AKA exchange, compare the challenge material to the response, and identify where the loop diverges from the expected sequence.
Why it works: One precise trace often exposes the mismatch faster than broad platform changes or generic scaling.
Verify: The traced failure mode is eliminated after the fix and new attempts no longer repeat the same challenge pair.
Operator hint: Keep one sanitized example exchange attached to the incident for future retrieval.

articles.title: Verify time synchronization on authentication and subscriber nodes
articles.summary: Clock skew can invalidate signatures, tokens, or nonce windows during registration.
content: When to use: Authentication failures began after node maintenance, a restart, or an infrastructure event affecting time sync.
Action: Compare time offset across auth service pods, subscriber backends, and any security gateway in the path.
Why it works: Even small skew can break challenge validation or token freshness checks in tightly bounded auth flows.
Verify: Offsets return to acceptable limits and the authentication error rate drops without another app-level change.
Follow-up: Add node time drift to incident evidence because it is easy to overlook in SIP-only analysis.

articles.title: Clear stale cached 401 state after the backend fault is fixed
articles.summary: Some auth incidents persist because one cache keeps serving an old reject state.
content: When to use: The subscriber backend has recovered, but one gateway or service replica still behaves as if credentials are invalid.
Action: Invalidate the stale auth cache or replace the affected pod so a fresh subscriber lookup can occur.
Why it works: Clearing stale reject state lets the system observe the recovered backend instead of replaying old failures.
Verify: Successful registrations appear immediately after cache eviction and no longer depend on landing on a different replica.
Risk: Avoid global cache flushes during peak traffic unless you know the backend can handle the refill load.

articles.title: Confirm authentication policy routing and realm selection
articles.summary: A realm or policy misroute can send valid users through the wrong auth path.
content: When to use: Authentication rejects correlate with a specific realm, domain, or onboarding path after a config change.
Action: Validate realm mapping, policy routing, and tenant selection so the request reaches the correct authentication backend.
Why it works: Correct credentials still fail if the request is evaluated against the wrong policy or subscriber domain.
Verify: Failures disappear for the affected realm without changing subscriber data or scaling the service.
Rollback trigger: A recent policy map or routing rule update that changed which backend owns the realm.

articles.title: Rollback the recent authentication configuration change when the blast radius is broad
articles.summary: A wide auth outage after a rollout is often best stabilized by a quick targeted rollback.
content: When to use: Failure volume jumps immediately after an auth service rollout and affects multiple subscriber groups at once.
Action: Compare the current and prior revision, roll back the smallest suspect change set, and keep evidence of the failing config for later review.
Why it works: Authentication changes often sit on the critical path, so fast rollback minimizes operator guesswork during a live incident.
Verify: The reject rate returns to baseline on the prior revision and new challenge loops stop forming.
Aftercare: Reproduce the failed change in a lower-risk environment before attempting another rollout.
